question: Bellman–Ford relaxes every edge V−1 times. Why exactly V−1?

choices.1: Each edge can only be safely relaxed once per vertex, and there are V−1 vertices other than the source
choices.2: Relaxing fewer than V−1 times risks integer overflow when summing negative weights
choices.3: A shortest path in a graph of V vertices uses at most V−1 edges, so V−1 passes give every shortest path the chance to fully form
choices.4: The algorithm must traverse all V−1 spanning-tree edges exactly once to guarantee correctness

question: After the V−1 relaxation passes, Bellman–Ford runs one additional pass. What does it mean if some distance still decreases during this extra pass?

choices.1: The source vertex was chosen incorrectly and the algorithm must restart
choices.2: The graph is disconnected, so some vertices remain unreachable
choices.3: The earlier passes terminated early and simply need to be repeated
choices.4: A negative cycle reachable from the source exists, so no well-defined shortest path exists for the affected vertices

question: What distinguishes Bellman–Ford from Dijkstra's algorithm?

choices.1: It runs faster on dense graphs because it avoids a priority queue
choices.2: It computes all-pairs shortest paths instead of single-source
choices.3: It correctly handles negative edge weights and can detect reachable negative cycles
choices.4: It still finds an optimal path even when edge weights change during execution

question: What is the worst-case time complexity of Bellman–Ford?

choices.1: O((V + E) log V)
choices.2: O(V · E)
choices.3: O(V²)
choices.4: O(E log V)

question: The implementation tracks an `updated` flag and breaks out early when a full pass makes no changes. What does this optimization accomplish?

choices.1: It lowers the worst-case complexity to O(E log V)
choices.2: It guarantees the algorithm finishes in at most O(V) passes regardless of graph shape
choices.3: It is required for correctness; without it, negative cycles could not be detected
choices.4: It lets typical graphs settle and terminate early, but the worst case stays O(V · E)

question: Relaxation is the algorithm's only primitive. For a directed edge u → v of weight w, when is dist[v] lowered?

choices.1: When dist[u] is not infinite and dist[u] + w < dist[v]
choices.2: When dist[v] + w < dist[u], regardless of whether dist[u] is finite
choices.3: Whenever w is negative, since a negative edge always shortens a path
choices.4: When v has not yet been touched during the current pass

question: What invariant holds after the i-th relaxation pass over all edges?

choices.1: Exactly i vertices have reached their final shortest-path distance
choices.2: Every shortest path that uses at most i edges has its correct distance recorded
choices.3: The i vertices closest to the source are finalized, as in Dijkstra
choices.4: All paths of length exactly i are discovered, but shorter ones may still be wrong

question: Given the edges 0→1 (5), 1→2 (−2), and 0→2 (10) with source 0, what is the final shortest distance to vertex 2?

choices.1: 3, via the path 0→1→2 (5 + (−2))
choices.2: 10, via the direct edge 0→2
choices.3: 5, since that is the cost to first reach vertex 1
choices.4: Infinity, because the negative edge forms a cycle

question: Bellman–Ford's negative-cycle detection makes it the standard tool for which of the following?

choices.1: Computing minimum spanning trees in graphs with negative weights
choices.2: Finding strongly connected components in a directed graph
choices.3: Maximizing flow through a network with capacity constraints
choices.4: Detecting currency-arbitrage opportunities and serving as the per-vertex subroutine inside Johnson's all-pairs shortest-path algorithm

question: How does the SPFA variant relate to standard Bellman–Ford?

choices.1: It improves the worst-case bound to O(E + V log V) by using a Fibonacci heap
choices.2: It processes edges in sorted weight order, like Kruskal's algorithm
choices.3: It only queues the vertices whose distance just changed — often faster in practice but with the same O(V · E) worst case
choices.4: It drops negative-cycle detection in exchange for guaranteed linear time

question: What is the space complexity of Bellman–Ford, and what does it store?

choices.1: O(E), one entry per edge
choices.2: O(V), one distance value per vertex
choices.3: O(V²), an adjacency matrix of distances
choices.4: O(V · E), one distance per edge per pass

question: Binary lifting precomputes only Θ(log n) different jump lengths per node, yet can answer a jump of any length k. Which property of integers makes that sufficient?

choices.1: Any k can be decomposed into the set bits of its binary representation, so composing the precomputed 2^i jumps reproduces a jump of length k
choices.2: Every tree has at most log n distinct depths, so only log n jump lengths can ever occur
choices.3: Tree paths always have a power-of-two length, so non-power-of-two jumps are never requested
choices.4: Applying the single parent pointer log n times can reach any ancestor

question: Let up[k][v] be the 2^k-th ancestor of v. Which recurrence correctly builds row k from the row below it?

choices.3: up[k][v] = up[k-1][v] + up[k-1][v]

question: Why does the doubling recurrence up[k][v] = up[k-1][up[k-1][v]] give the 2^k-th ancestor?

choices.1: It jumps 2^(k-1) steps and then 2^(k-1) more, and 2^(k-1)+2^(k-1) = 2^k
choices.2: It jumps 2^(k-1) steps and then 1 more, giving 2^(k-1)+1 levels
choices.3: It multiplies the parent index by k, scaling the jump to 2^k
choices.4: It averages two adjacent rows, which doubles the implied depth

question: What are the time and space costs of building the binary-lifting table for an n-node tree?

choices.1: O(n) time and O(n) space
choices.2: O(n^2) time and O(n^2) space
choices.3: O(n log n) time and O(n log n) space
choices.4: O(n log n) time but only O(n) space

question: How many rows (power-of-two levels) does the table need for an n-node tree?

choices.1: Exactly n rows
choices.2: ceil(log2 n) + 1 rows
choices.3: Exactly 2 rows, the parent and grandparent
choices.4: n / log2 n rows

question: kthAncestor loops over bit positions i and does `if (k & (1<<i)) v = up[i][v];`. Why is a single k-th-ancestor query O(log n)?

choices.1: Each up[i][v] lookup internally recurses log n times
choices.2: The loop always runs exactly n / log n iterations
choices.3: k < n has at most ceil(log2 n) bits, so at most that many power-of-two jumps are taken across the log n rows
choices.4: Each jump halves the remaining distance, like a binary search on edge count

question: In LCA, phase 1 lifts the deeper node up by k = |depth(u) - depth(v)| levels. What is its purpose, and what does it mean if the two nodes coincide afterward?

choices.1: It moves both nodes to the root; coinciding means the tree is a single path
choices.2: It equalises their depths; coinciding means the shallower node was an ancestor of the deeper one and is itself the LCA
choices.3: It makes the two nodes siblings; coinciding means the tree is malformed
choices.4: It finds the midpoint of the path; coinciding means they were the same node to begin with

question: In phase 2 of the LCA algorithm, iterating k from the highest power down, when are BOTH u and v jumped up by 2^k?

choices.1: Only when up[k][u] == up[k][v], i.e. their 2^k-th ancestors already match
choices.2: On every iteration unconditionally, until k reaches 0
choices.3: Whenever 2^k is less than or equal to the remaining depth difference
choices.4: Only when up[k][u] != up[k][v], i.e. their 2^k-th ancestors still differ

question: After phase 2 of LCA finishes, why is the returned answer up[0][u] (u's parent) rather than u itself?

choices.1: The greedy lifting keeps u and v strictly below the LCA, stopping when they are distinct children of it, so their shared parent up[0][u] is the LCA
choices.2: u has been lifted exactly one level too high, and up[0] corrects that off-by-one
choices.3: up[0][u] is always the root, the only guaranteed common ancestor
choices.4: u and v are now equal, and up[0] retrieves the deepest distinct ancestor

question: The rows up[k][v] for k >= 1 are filled inside the DFS, before recursing into v's children. Why is that ordering correct?

choices.1: Because v's children are visited first, so their columns are ready to compose
choices.2: Because DFS visits nodes in increasing index order, so dependencies are always met
choices.3: In a pre-order traversal every ancestor of v is visited before v, so the lower-row entries that up[k][v] composes are already complete
choices.4: Because the recurrence reads only v's own column, which never depends on any other node

question: The build sets the root's parent to itself (up[0][root] = root). What does this sentinel accomplish?

choices.1: It marks the root so the LCA routine can detect when both nodes reach the top
choices.2: Any jump that would go above the root harmlessly stays at the root, so over-jumps never read an invalid node
choices.3: It forces the root's depth to be computed as 1 instead of 0
choices.4: It lets kthAncestor return -1 when k exceeds the node's depth

question: Per the comparison of LCA methods, when is Euler tour + sparse-table RMQ the better choice than binary lifting?

choices.1: When all queries are known up front and can be answered offline
choices.2: When you also need k-th-ancestor and level-ancestor queries from the same structure
choices.3: When you specifically need O(1) queries and can accept more code for an LCA-only structure
choices.4: When preprocessing must be O(n) rather than O(n log n)

question: Why is binary lifting described as an 'online' LCA method while Tarjan's Union-Find approach is 'offline'?

choices.1: Binary lifting answers queries as they arrive one at a time, whereas Tarjan's requires all queries to be known in advance
choices.2: Binary lifting runs over a live network connection while Tarjan's runs locally
choices.3: Binary lifting mutates the tree during each query while Tarjan's leaves it unchanged
choices.4: Tarjan's is online because Union-Find processes unions incrementally; binary lifting must batch its queries

question: Binary lifting generalises to any functional graph (each node has a single succ(v) pointer). With edge data stored beside the 2^k jumps, which extra query can the same power-of-two decomposition answer?

choices.1: The exact number of distinct cycles reachable from a node
choices.2: Aggregates like the min/max/sum of edge weights along a path, combined over the same 2^k segments being jumped
choices.3: The shortest path between any two nodes in an arbitrary weighted graph
choices.4: The total number of nodes in the subtree rooted at v

question: Dijkstra is greedy: it always finalises the unvisited vertex with the smallest tentative distance. Why is a vertex's distance final the moment it is popped from the min-heap?

choices.1: Because the heap guarantees no other vertex can ever later be pushed with a smaller key
choices.2: Because edges are non-negative, so any later path to it can only add non-negative weight and can never beat the distance it was popped with
choices.3: Because each vertex is pushed to the heap exactly once, so the first pop is the only pop
choices.4: Because relaxation always processes a vertex's edges in ascending weight order

question: Why does Dijkstra's correctness require non-negative edge weights?

choices.1: A negative edge could make a cheaper route to an already-settled vertex appear later, breaking the greedy finality invariant and silently producing wrong answers
choices.2: Negative weights cause the min-heap to extract vertices in the wrong order because of integer overflow
choices.3: Negative weights make the distance array hold negative values, which a min-heap cannot order
choices.4: Negative weights force the algorithm into an infinite loop re-relaxing the source

question: What is the time complexity of Dijkstra implemented with a binary min-heap?

choices.1: O(V * E)
choices.3: O((V + E) log V)
choices.4: O(E + V log V)

question: Python's heapq has no decrease-key operation. How does the implementation handle a vertex whose tentative distance improves during relaxation?

choices.1: It locates the old entry via an index map and decreases its key in place
choices.2: It removes the old entry from the middle of the heap and reinserts the smaller one
choices.3: It updates the distance in place and re-heapifies the whole heap in O(V)
choices.4: It pushes a new, smaller (distance, node) entry and leaves the old one, so a vertex may appear several times (lazy deletion)

question: What is the purpose of the guard `if current_dist > dist[u]: continue` at the top of the pop loop?

choices.1: It discards stale duplicate heap entries so each vertex's edges are relaxed only once, at its final distance
choices.2: It detects a negative-weight edge and aborts the algorithm
choices.3: It stops the source vertex from being relaxed against itself
choices.4: It terminates the loop early once every reachable vertex is settled

question: Because duplicates are pushed instead of using decrease-key, how many entries can the heap hold, and what complexity bound does that yield?

choices.1: O(V) entries, giving O(V log V)
choices.2: O(E) entries, giving O((V + E) log V)
choices.3: O(V^2) entries, giving O(V^2 log V)
choices.4: O(V + E) entries, giving O((V + E)^2)

question: While relaxing edge (u, v, weight) with u's settled distance current_dist, under what condition is dist[v] updated?

choices.1: Whenever v has not yet been popped from the heap, regardless of distance
choices.2: When current_dist + weight > dist[v]
choices.3: When weight < dist[u], regardless of dist[v]
choices.4: When current_dist + weight < dist[v], i.e. a shorter tentative path is found

question: How is the dist[] array initialised before the main loop runs?

choices.1: Every vertex, including the source, set to 0
choices.2: The source set to 0 and every other vertex set to infinity
choices.3: The source set to infinity and every other vertex set to 0
choices.4: Every vertex set to infinity, including the source

question: How does the implementation reconstruct the actual shortest path, not just its length?

choices.1: It stores the full path so far inside each heap entry alongside the distance
choices.2: It re-runs Dijkstra from the target and intersects the two distance arrays
choices.3: It records each vertex's predecessor during relaxation, then walks the parent chain backward from the target and reverses it
choices.4: It sorts all vertices by final distance; consecutive vertices form the path

question: Per the Dijkstra vs Bellman-Ford comparison, which capability does Bellman-Ford have that Dijkstra lacks?

choices.1: Detecting negative-weight cycles
choices.2: Running in O((V + E) log V) time
choices.3: Using a greedy min-heap strategy
choices.4: Computing single-source shortest paths

question: For a dense graph where E is roughly V^2, which Dijkstra variant can beat the binary-heap version, and at what complexity?

choices.1: A Fibonacci-heap Dijkstra at O(E + V log V)
choices.2: An array-based (linear-scan) Dijkstra at O(V^2)
choices.3: Bellman-Ford at O(V * E)
choices.4: Plain BFS at O(V + E)

question: What does using a Fibonacci heap give Dijkstra, and why is it rarely used in practice?

choices.1: The theoretical O(E + V log V) bound, but its constant factors make it rarely worth it
choices.2: An O((V + E) log V) bound with simpler code than a binary heap
choices.3: An O(V^2) bound, making it ideal for dense graphs
choices.4: Support for negative edges, at the cost of higher memory use

question: According to the note, what already yields shortest paths in O(V + E) on an unweighted graph, without needing Dijkstra?

choices.1: Array-based Dijkstra
choices.2: Bellman-Ford
choices.3: Plain BFS
choices.4: A Fibonacci-heap Dijkstra

question: How does the note relate the A* search algorithm to Dijkstra?

choices.1: A* is Dijkstra plus a heuristic
choices.2: A* is Bellman-Ford with a min-heap
choices.3: A* is Dijkstra run backward from the target
choices.4: A* is Dijkstra restricted to graphs with a zero heuristic

question: What precisely is a strongly connected component (SCC) of a directed graph?

choices.1: A maximal set of vertices in which every pair u, v has both a directed path u->v and a directed path v->u
choices.2: A maximal set of vertices all reachable from one chosen source vertex
choices.3: Any directed cycle in the graph, including ones that overlap
choices.4: A set of vertices that has no incoming edges from outside the set

question: How does Kosaraju's algorithm find all SCCs, and at what cost?

choices.1: A single DFS pass tracking discovery and low-link values, in O(V + E)
choices.2: Two DFS passes - one on G and one on its transpose G^T - in O(V + E)
choices.3: One DFS pass plus a union-find structure, in O((V + E) * alpha)
choices.4: Repeated BFS from every vertex, in O(V * (V + E))

question: In Kosaraju's first DFS pass over the original graph G, in what order are vertices pushed onto the stack?

choices.1: Discovery order - a vertex is pushed the moment it is first visited
choices.2: Ascending vertex-index order
choices.3: Finish-time order - a vertex is pushed once all of its descendants are done
choices.4: The order in which edges were inserted into the adjacency list

question: What is the transpose graph G^T that the second DFS pass runs on?

choices.1: The same graph with every directed edge reversed
choices.2: The same graph with all edge weights negated
choices.3: The undirected version of G
choices.4: The complement graph containing exactly the edges G lacks

question: Why does processing vertices in reverse finish order on the transpose keep each DFS tree confined to a single SCC?

choices.1: The transpose has no edges between SCCs at all, so traversal is physically confined
choices.2: Reverse finish order happens to visit vertices in ascending index order, isolating components
choices.3: Each SCC becomes a fully disconnected island in the transpose
choices.4: Edges that leave an SCC in G point into already-finished components in G^T, so a traversal cannot leak into another SCC

question: Relative to the condensation DAG, what ordering of the SCCs do the first pass's finishing times produce?

choices.1: Reverse topological order of the condensation DAG
choices.2: Exact topological order of the condensation DAG
choices.3: Order of increasing SCC size
choices.4: A random permutation unrelated to the DAG structure

question: What is the condensation of a directed graph, and what structural property does it always have?

choices.1: Remove every back edge; the result is always strongly connected
choices.2: Collapse each SCC to a single node; the result is always a DAG (acyclic)
choices.3: Collapse each SCC to a single node; the result is always a tree
choices.4: Merge all sink vertices; the result is always bipartite

question: What is the overall time and space complexity of Kosaraju's algorithm?

choices.1: O(V * E) time and O(V) space
choices.2: O((V + E) log V) time and O(V + E) space
choices.3: O(V^2) time and O(V^2) space
choices.4: O(V + E) time and O(V + E) space

question: What is the main practical difference between Kosaraju's and Tarjan's SCC algorithms?

choices.1: Kosaraju uses two DFS passes and needs the transpose; Tarjan uses one pass with discovery/low-link values and no transpose
choices.2: Kosaraju is O(V + E) while Tarjan is O(V * E)
choices.3: Tarjan needs the transpose graph while Kosaraju does not
choices.4: Kosaraju detects negative cycles while Tarjan only finds SCCs

question: How are SCCs used to decide the satisfiability of a 2-SAT instance via its implication graph?

choices.1: It is satisfiable iff no variable shares an SCC with its own negation
choices.2: It is satisfiable iff the condensation has exactly one source component
choices.3: It is satisfiable iff every variable and its negation lie in the same SCC
choices.4: It is satisfiable iff the implication graph is already a DAG

question: When reading each edge (u, v), the code runs `g[u].append(v); gt[v].append(u)`. What does the second line build?

choices.1: A duplicate of g so both passes can run concurrently
choices.2: The transpose adjacency list, recording the reversed edge v->u
choices.3: An undirected adjacency list combining both directions
choices.4: The condensation edges between components

question: What practical caveat does the note raise about the recursive implementation on large graphs?

choices.1: The recursion can double-count SCCs, so a visited check must run after each pass
choices.2: The transpose must be rebuilt inside each recursive call, which is slow
choices.3: The recursion can blow the call stack even with a raised limit, so convert both passes to explicit/iterative stacks
choices.4: Recursion makes the algorithm O(V^2) instead of O(V + E)

question: In the second pass, popping an unvisited vertex from the reversed finish order and running a fresh DFS on G^T yields a DFS tree that represents what?

choices.1: Exactly one SCC
choices.2: One edge of the condensation DAG
choices.3: A topological layer that may span several SCCs
choices.4: A path from a source SCC to a sink SCC

question: When building the condensation in code, which original edges (u, v) become edges of the component DAG?

choices.1: Only those where comp_id[u] == comp_id[v] - edges within the same SCC
choices.2: Only those where comp_id[u] != comp_id[v] - edges crossing between different SCCs
choices.3: Every edge, with duplicates removed afterward
choices.4: Only edges incident to a singleton SCC

question: How does a vertex that is not mutually reachable with any other vertex appear in Kosaraju's output?

choices.1: It is omitted from the output entirely
choices.2: It is merged into the nearest larger SCC
choices.3: As a singleton SCC - a component of size one
choices.4: It is reported only in the condensation, not in the SCC list

question: What does a topological ordering of a directed graph guarantee?

choices.1: For every directed edge u->v, u appears before v in the ordering
choices.2: Every node appears in ascending index order
choices.3: Each node is adjacent in the order to all of its neighbours
choices.4: Nodes are listed by decreasing indegree

question: Under what condition does a topological order of a graph exist?

choices.1: If and only if the graph is connected
choices.2: If and only if the graph is a DAG (has no cycle)
choices.3: If and only if every node has indegree at least 1
choices.4: If and only if the graph is undirected

question: In Kahn's algorithm, which nodes are placed into the queue initially?

choices.1: Every node with outdegree 0
choices.2: An arbitrary single start node
choices.3: Every node with indegree 0
choices.4: The node with the highest indegree

question: How does Kahn's algorithm detect that the graph has a cycle?

choices.1: The emitted order ends up shorter than n (some nodes are never enqueued)
choices.2: The queue grows larger than n entries
choices.3: A node is popped twice from the queue
choices.4: An indegree value goes negative

question: After popping a node in Kahn's algorithm, what is done for each of its neighbours?

choices.1: Their indegree is incremented and they are immediately emitted
choices.2: Their indegree is decremented, and any whose indegree hits 0 is pushed to the queue
choices.3: They are pushed to the queue unconditionally
choices.4: Their outdegree is decremented and the node is revisited

question: How does the DFS-based topological sort produce its ordering?

choices.1: It emits nodes in discovery (pre-order) order
choices.2: It pushes each node after all its neighbours are processed, then reverses the result
choices.3: It emits nodes in ascending finish time without reversing
choices.4: It sorts nodes by indegree after a single DFS

question: In the DFS approach with three-colour marking (0 unvisited, 1 visiting, 2 done), what indicates a cycle?

choices.1: Reaching a node coloured 2 (done)
choices.2: Reaching a node coloured 1 (on the current recursion path) - a back edge
choices.3: Finishing DFS with some nodes still coloured 0
choices.4: Any node whose colour changes from 1 to 2

question: What are the time and space complexities of both Kahn's and the DFS-based topological sort?

choices.1: O(V + E) time and O(V + E) space
choices.2: O(V log V) time and O(V) space
choices.3: O(V * E) time and O(V) space
choices.4: O(V^2) time and O(V^2) space

question: Which task is Kahn's algorithm naturally suited to, per the comparison?

choices.1: Producing the lexicographically smallest order by using a heap instead of a plain queue
choices.2: Supplying the reverse-finish order reused by Kosaraju's algorithm
choices.3: Detecting cycles via low-link values
choices.4: Finding shortest paths in weighted DAGs

question: What by-product of the DFS-based topological sort is reused elsewhere in the notes?

choices.1: An indegree histogram used by rate limiters
choices.2: The reverse-finish order reused by Kosaraju's SCC algorithm
choices.3: A min-heap of nodes used by Dijkstra
choices.4: A union-find forest used by Kruskal

question: Which two operations does a Union-Find (DSU) structure support?

choices.1: insert(x) and delete(x)
choices.2: find(x) - the representative of x's set - and union(x, y) - merge two sets
choices.3: push(x) and pop()
choices.4: rank(x) and select(k)

question: What does the path-compression optimisation do during a find?

choices.1: It points every node visited on the way to the root directly at the root, flattening the tree
choices.2: It always attaches the smaller tree under the larger one
choices.3: It caches the most recent query so repeats are O(1)
choices.4: It rebalances the tree to be a perfect binary tree

question: What does 'union by size/rank' achieve?

choices.1: It removes the need to store a parent array
choices.2: It keeps trees shallow by hanging the smaller (or shorter) tree under the larger one
choices.3: It guarantees every find is exactly O(1) worst case
choices.4: It detects cycles without comparing roots

question: With both path compression and union by size/rank, what is the amortised cost per operation?

choices.1: O(log n)
choices.2: O(1) worst case, never amortised
choices.3: O(alpha(n)) amortised - effectively constant
choices.4: O(sqrt n) amortised

question: Without any optimisation, what is the worst-case cost of find, and why?

choices.1: O(n), because the tree can degenerate into a linked list (a chain)
choices.2: O(log n), because the tree stays balanced automatically
choices.3: O(1), because parent pointers are followed once
choices.4: O(n^2), because every node is re-linked on each find

question: Per the note, what is the find worst case if you apply ONLY one of the two optimisations (size/rank alone, or path compression alone)?

choices.1: O(alpha(n))
choices.3: O(n)
choices.4: O(log n)

question: In the implementation, union returns False when the two roots already match. How is that used?

choices.1: As the cycle-detection hook: in Kruskal's MST or undirected cycle detection it means the edge would close a cycle
choices.2: To signal that the structure has run out of memory
choices.3: To trigger a rebuild with path compression
choices.4: To indicate the two elements are in different sets

question: How is a set's representative identified in the parent-array representation?

choices.1: It is the node with the largest index in the set
choices.2: It is the root - the node that is its own parent
choices.3: It is whichever node was inserted first
choices.4: It is stored in a separate representative[] array

question: What is alpha(n) in the Union-Find complexity, and roughly how large does it get?

choices.1: The binary logarithm; it reaches ~20 for a million elements
choices.2: The inverse Ackermann function; it stays <= 4 for any practical input
choices.3: The golden ratio; it is a fixed constant 1.618
choices.4: The harmonic number; it grows like ln n

question: Which algorithm uses Union-Find to add an edge only when its endpoints aren't already connected?

choices.1: Dijkstra's shortest path
choices.2: Kruskal's minimum spanning tree
choices.3: Kahn's topological sort
choices.4: Bellman-Ford

question: What risk does the recursive find have on very deep inputs, and what is the suggested fix?

choices.1: It can overflow the call stack before compression flattens the tree; use an iterative two-pass find
choices.2: It can return the wrong root; add a visited set
choices.3: It can loop forever on cycles; add cycle detection
choices.4: It can corrupt the size array; recompute sizes after each union

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/Probabilistic Data Structures/Bloom Filter.md
question: What error profile does a standard Bloom filter have?

choices.1: It can have false negatives but never false positives
choices.2: It can have both false positives and false negatives
choices.3: It can have false positives but never false negatives
choices.4: It is always exact, like a hash set

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/Probabilistic Data Structures/Bloom Filter.md
question: How should each answer from a Bloom filter be interpreted?

choices.1: 'No' means definitely absent; 'yes' means probably present
choices.2: 'No' means probably absent; 'yes' means definitely present
choices.3: Both answers are only probabilistic
choices.4: 'Yes' means definitely present; 'no' means definitely absent

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/Probabilistic Data Structures/Bloom Filter.md
question: What does INSERT(x) do in a Bloom filter with k hash functions?

choices.1: Appends x to a backing list and increments a counter
choices.2: Sets the bit at each of the k hashed positions to 1
choices.3: Clears the bit at one hashed position
choices.4: Stores x at hash_1(x) and probes linearly on collision

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/Probabilistic Data Structures/Bloom Filter.md
question: How does QUERY(x) decide its answer?

choices.1: If any of x's k bits is 0, answer 'no'; if all k are 1, answer 'probably yes'
choices.2: If a majority of x's k bits are 1, answer 'yes'
choices.3: If the first hashed bit is 1, answer 'yes'
choices.4: If all k bits are 0, answer 'probably yes'

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/Probabilistic Data Structures/Bloom Filter.md
question: Why does a standard Bloom filter not support deletion?

choices.1: Because clearing an element's bits could unset bits shared with other elements, corrupting them
choices.2: Because the hash functions are one-way and can't be inverted
choices.3: Because the bit array is stored on disk and is read-only
choices.4: Because deletion would create false negatives only for the deleted key, which is acceptable

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/Probabilistic Data Structures/Bloom Filter.md
question: Which structure supports deletion that a standard Bloom filter cannot?

choices.1: A Counting Bloom filter (counters instead of single bits)
choices.2: A HyperLogLog
choices.3: A Count-Min Sketch
choices.4: A plain bit array with k=1

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/Probabilistic Data Structures/Bloom Filter.md
question: The false-positive probability is approximately (1 - e^(-k*n/m))^k. What is the qualitative trade-off in choosing k?

choices.1: k too small under-fingerprints (collisions); k too large fills the array too fast - there is an optimum
choices.2: Larger k always lowers the false-positive rate without limit
choices.3: k has no effect on the false-positive rate, only m does
choices.4: Smaller k always lowers the false-positive rate without limit

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/Probabilistic Data Structures/Bloom Filter.md
question: Given a target false-positive rate p and expected count n, what is the optimal number of hashes k?

choices.1: k = n / m
choices.2: k = (m / n) * ln 2  (about 0.693 * m/n)
choices.3: k = m * n
choices.4: k = ln(1/p) * n

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/Probabilistic Data Structures/Bloom Filter.md
question: What are the time and space complexities of a Bloom filter?

choices.1: O(k) per insert/query and O(m) bits of space, independent of element size
choices.2: O(log n) per operation and O(n) space
choices.3: O(1) per operation and O(n) space for stored elements
choices.4: O(k) per operation and O(k*n) space

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/Probabilistic Data Structures/Bloom Filter.md
question: What is the canonical usage pattern for a Bloom filter (e.g. a per-SSTable filter in Cassandra/HBase)?

choices.1: A cheap, memory-resident pre-filter in front of an expensive exact check, where a 'no' lets you skip the expensive work
choices.2: A replacement for the authoritative store, since 'yes' is guaranteed correct
choices.3: A way to count how many times each key has been seen
choices.4: A sorted index that returns the keys in range

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/Probabilistic Data Structures/Bloom Filter.md
question: Which set operation is reliable on two Bloom filters that share the same m and k?

choices.1: Intersection, by ANDing the bit arrays
choices.2: Union, by ORing the bit arrays
choices.3: Difference, by XORing the bit arrays
choices.4: Neither union nor intersection is possible

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/Probabilistic Data Structures/Bloom Filter.md
question: What happens to a Bloom filter's false-positive rate if you keep inserting well past its design capacity n?

choices.1: It silently degrades toward 100% as more bits get set
choices.2: It stays fixed because m and k are constant
choices.3: It starts producing false negatives instead
choices.4: It automatically resizes m to compensate
